message.content: Adversaries may exploit the "Build Image on Host" technique to create container images directly on a host, circumventing defenses that monitor for malicious images from public registries. By sending a remote build request to the Docker API, they can use a Dockerfile to pull a benign base image, such as alpine, and build a custom image that includes malware from their command and control (C2) server. This custom image can then be deployed, often evading detection since the base image appears legitimate. The impact includes potential unauthorized access, data exfiltration, and further system compromise. Common exploitation methods involve leveraging Docker APIs and manipulating Dockerfiles. Mitigation strategies include restricting Docker API access, monitoring and validating Dockerfile contents, and employing robust image scanning and validation processes to detect anomalies in container images.
